name: Yemoja
title: Yoruba - Yemoja - Mother of Waters and Fertility
domains: Yeye Omo Eja (Mother Whose Children Are Fish)
epithets: Yeye Omo Eja (Mother Whose Children Are Fish)

relationships.consort: in many traditions. their union represents the union of water and land

description: Also known as Yemoja (Yemoja, Yemaya, Iemanja). Associated with Yeye Omo Eja (Mother Whose Children Are Fish), Mama Wata, Olokun Omo (Child of Olokun), Yemowo, Ibu. Known as Yeye Omo Eja (Mother Whose Children Are Fish). Symbolized by ocean waves.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to the Sea
worshipPractices.description: Devotees often make offerings of flowers, fruits, and other gifts to the ocean or bodies of water associated with Yemoja. These offerings are meant to show respect, seek blessings, and express gratitude for her protection and abundance.

worshipPractices.name: Divination and Consultation
worshipPractices.description: Priests and priestesses are consulted to interpret Yemoja's will through divination methods. These divinations guide followers in making decisions, understanding challenges, and seeking solutions in accordance with her teachings and guidance.

festivals.name: Yemoja Festival
festivals.timing: Celebrated annually, often around late August or early September
festivals.description: This festival involves processions to the sea, drumming, dancing, and offerings to Yemoja. Participants wear blue and white clothing, symbolizing her connection to the ocean, and celebrate her role as a mother and provider.

festivals.name: New Year's Eve Celebration
festivals.description: In some regions, particularly in Brazil, Yemoja is honored on New Year's Eve. People dressed in white gather on the beaches, launching flower-filled boats into the ocean as offerings, and making wishes for the coming year.

cultural.modernLegacy: Yemoja's influence is seen in contemporary art, music, and literature, particularly within the African diaspora. She is often depicted as a powerful and nurturing figure, representing both the strength and gentleness of the ocean and serving as a symbol of motherhood, fertility, and protection for many communities around the world.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are powerful goddesses associated with the ocean and motherhood. Amphitrite is the wife of Poseidon and queen of the sea, embodying the vastness and nurturing aspects of the ocean, similar to Yemoja.

Danu is considered the mother goddess of the Tuatha Dé Danann, representing fertility and abundance. While not directly tied to the ocean, her role as a primal mother figure parallels Yemoja's association with motherhood and the source of life.

Ganga is the goddess of the Ganges River, revered as a purifying and life-giving force. Both goddesses are associated with bodies of water that are vital for sustenance and have strong maternal connections to their people.
Salacia is the Roman goddess of saltwater and the wife of Neptune. Like Yemoja, she embodies the power and mystery of the ocean and is seen as a queen of the sea.

associations.name: Ocean
associations.significance: Yemoja is the orisha of the ocean and embodies its vastness and power.

associations.name: Fish
associations.significance: Yemoja is known as 'Mother Whose Children Are Fish', representing her nurturing role and connection to marine life.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Blue represents the depth and tranquility of the ocean, reflecting Yemoja's serene yet powerful nature.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon's connection to tides symbolizes Yemoja's influence over the waters and her maternal power.

associations.name: Motherhood
associations.significance: Yemoja is revered as the universal mother, embodying compassion, protection, and nurturing qualities.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is a sacred number often associated with Yemoja in rituals and offerings.

associations.name: Water Lily
associations.significance: Water lilies, growing in aquatic environments, symbolize purity, beauty, and Yemoja's connection to water.

companions.name: Oshun
companions.description: Oshun is Yemoja's sister and fellow Orisha. They often work together to provide and nurture humanity, with Oshun representing freshwater and beauty.

companions.name: Olokun
companions.description: Olokun is considered by some traditions to be the father of Yemoja. Their relationship represents the connection between the surface of the ocean and its unfathomable depths.

companions.name: Shango
companions.description: Shango is a powerful Orisha of thunder and lightning and in some traditions is the husband of Yemoja. Their relationship embodies a balance of strength and nurturing.

companions.name: Eja
companions.description: Eja (Fish) are considered her children or messengers. They embody her nurturing and life-giving qualities, as Yemoja is known as the 'Mother Whose Children Are Fish'.

companions.name: Egungun
companions.description: As a powerful mother figure, Yemoja is sometimes associated with the protection of ancestral spirits (Egungun). She provides a safe passage for them.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Yemoja's origins are deeply rooted in the creation myths of the Yoruba people. While variations exist across different lineages and regions, she is generally considered to be a primordial deity, often described as a daughter of Olokun, the god/goddess of the deep ocean. Some accounts portray her as a wife of Obatala, the creator god, and the mother of many Yoruba deities, including Ogun, Shango, and others. This maternal role earns her the title 'Yeye Omo Eja,' meaning 'Mother Whose Children Are Fish,' highlighting her association with fertility and the abundance of life that originates from the waters. Her birth, in some narratives, is intertwined with the creation of lagoons and rivers, suggesting her physical manifestation is intrinsically linked to the earth's waterways. The story of her transformation from a river deity to an ocean goddess speaks to the fluidity and adaptability of Yoruba beliefs, influenced by the geographical shifts and migrations of the Yoruba people. Oral traditions, passed down through generations of priests and devotees, are crucial in preserving these diverse accounts of her origin.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples
extendedContent.content: Worship of Yemoja is widespread throughout the Yoruba diaspora, particularly in Nigeria, Brazil (as Iemanjá), and Cuba (as Yemaya). Her shrines and temples are typically located near bodies of water, especially the ocean, rivers, and lagoons, reflecting her dominion. Offerings to Yemoja often include fruits, flowers (especially white roses), perfumes, and other items associated with femininity and beauty. Devotees seek her blessings for fertility, safe childbirth, family well-being, and protection from the dangers of the sea. Annual festivals are held in her honor, marked by vibrant processions, drumming, dancing, and ritual cleansing. In Brazil, the most famous celebration takes place on New Year's Eve, when offerings are cast into the ocean to appease Iemanjá and seek her blessings for the coming year. These festivals serve as a powerful expression of faith, community solidarity, and cultural identity, connecting devotees to their ancestral roots and the life-giving force of Yemoja. The rituals surrounding her worship vary across different regions and lineages, reflecting the adaptability and syncretism inherent in Yoruba religious practices.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation
extendedContent.content: Yemoja's iconography varies across different regions and traditions, but she is generally depicted as a beautiful and powerful woman, often dressed in blue and white, colors associated with the ocean. She is frequently portrayed with flowing hair, symbolizing the movement of water, and adorned with coral beads and seashells. Her symbols include the cowrie shell, representing wealth and fertility, and the moon, reflecting her connection to feminine cycles and intuition. In some depictions, she carries a fan or a mirror, symbolizing her ability to reflect the truth and reveal hidden aspects of life. In Brazil, statues of Iemanjá often show her emerging from the ocean waves, holding a sword or a scepter, signifying her power and authority. Her representation in art and sculpture reflects her multifaceted nature as a nurturing mother, a fierce protector, and a powerful force of nature. These visual representations serve as tangible reminders of her presence and influence in the lives of her devotees.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content: Yemoja plays a crucial role in the Yoruba understanding of the cosmic order. As the mother of many deities, she is seen as a source of life and creation, embodying the principles of fertility, abundance, and nurturing. Her dominion over the waters connects her to the flow of life, both physical and spiritual. She is believed to possess the power to cleanse and purify, washing away negativity and promoting healing. Her association with the ocean also links her to the realm of the ancestors, as the sea is seen as a gateway to the spirit world. The ebb and flow of the tides are often interpreted as a reflection of her moods and emotions, highlighting her connection to the natural rhythms of the universe. By maintaining balance and harmony within the waters, Yemoja ensures the well-being of humanity and the continuation of life. Her role underscores the Yoruba belief in the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of respecting the power of nature.
